summary: Type 2 diabetes is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and progressive beta-cell dysfunction. First-line treatment is metformin unless contraindicated, combined with lifestyle modification. GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT2 inhibitors are preferred add-on therapies in patients with cardiovascular or renal comorbidities.
monitoring: HbA1c every 3 months until stable, then every 6 months

summary: Hypertension is defined as sustained blood pressure ≥130/80 mmHg. First-line agents include ACE inhibitors, ARBs, calcium channel blockers, and thiazide diuretics. Target BP is <130/80 mmHg for most adults. Lifestyle modifications (sodium restriction, weight loss, exercise) are recommended alongside pharmacotherapy.
monitoring: blood pressure at every clinical encounter

summary: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease characterized by variable airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. Stepwise therapy begins with short-acting beta-agonists (SABA) for symptom relief; inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) are the cornerstone of maintenance therapy. Biologics (anti-IL-5, anti-IL-4/13) are reserved for severe eosinophilic asthma.
monitoring: symptom control assessment (ACT score) at each visit
peak flow monitoring for unstable disease

aliases: heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
summary: Heart failure is a clinical syndrome of dyspnea and fluid retention due to structural or functional cardiac impairment. HFrEF (EF <40%) management includes ACE inhibitors/ARBs, beta-blockers, MRAs, and SGLT2 inhibitors — the 'fantastic four'. Diuretics relieve congestion but do not improve survival.
monitoring: BNP or NT-proBNP at diagnosis and with decompensation
renal function and electrolytes within 1-2 weeks of medication changes

summary: COPD is a progressive obstructive lung disease caused primarily by cigarette smoking. Treatment follows the GOLD staging system. Bronchodilators (LAMA, LABA) are the foundation of maintenance therapy. ICS are added in patients with frequent exacerbations or eosinophilia. Smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention.

Atrial fibrillation is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia, characterized by irregular atrial activity. Management involves rate control, rhythm control, and anticoagulation for stroke prevention. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score guides anticoagulation decisions. DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban) are preferred over warfarin.
monitoring: annual thyroid, renal, and liver function on DOAC

summary: CKD is defined by GFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² or markers of kidney damage for >3 months. Management targets underlying causes (diabetes, hypertension) and slows progression with ACE inhibitors/ARBs and SGLT2 inhibitors. Dietary protein restriction and phosphate management are relevant in advanced stages.
interventions: dietary protein restriction in advanced CKD
monitoring: eGFR and urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio every 3-6 months
electrolytes, bicarbonate, phosphate, PTH in CKD 3b+

summary: Hypothyroidism results from insufficient thyroid hormone production, most commonly due to Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Levothyroxine (T4) is the treatment of choice, dosed by weight and adjusted to achieve a TSH within the reference range. Older patients and those with cardiac disease require lower starting doses.
monitoring: TSH at 6-8 weeks after dose change, then annually once stable

summary: Major depressive disorder is characterized by persistent low mood, anhedonia, and neurovegetative symptoms lasting ≥2 weeks. SSRIs are first-line pharmacotherapy. Psychotherapy (CBT) is equally effective for mild-to-moderate depression and may be combined with medication. Treatment-resistant cases may require augmentation or referral to psychiatry.
monitoring: PHQ-9 at each visit to track symptom severity
assess for suicidality at every encounter
evaluate response at 4-8 weeks after initiating treatment

summary: Dyslipidemia encompasses elevated LDL-C, triglycerides, or low HDL-C. Statins are the cornerstone of LDL-lowering therapy and have robust cardiovascular outcome data. Ezetimibe and PCSK9 inhibitors are added for very-high-risk patients not at LDL goal. Lifestyle modification (diet, exercise) is always recommended.
monitoring: fasting lipid panel 4-12 weeks after initiating statin, then annually
